nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of clear lagoons and seaward reefs; generally found at depths of 1–73 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on coral polyps, sponges, molluscs, bryozoans, tunicates, crustaceans, and algae in the wild. In captivity, has been known to accept squid, krill, clams, shrimp, and other meaty foods. Hard-shelled prey such as clams and crabs are generally advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like teeth.

summary: A large, bold Indo-Pacific puffer available in black-spotted, mottled, and golden color phases; generally considered semi-aggressive and best kept singly in a large fish-only system with robust tankmates.
careNotes:
Arothron meleagris occurs across the Indo-Pacific and eastern Pacific and tends to inhabit coral-rich reef and lagoon environments. Three naturally occurring color phases have been described — a dark blue-to-black phase with white spots, a mottled yellow-and-black phase, and an all-yellow (golden) phase — though color-phase changes are rarely observed in captivity. The dark blue-to-black phase with white spots is sometimes sold in the aquarium trade specifically as the "Midnight Arothron Puffer," reflecting its darker overall coloration; it is not a distinct species or morph but simply the same Arothron meleagris in its darkest naturally occurring color phase.

This species has a distinctive beak-like structure formed by fused teeth that continues to grow throughout life; hard-shelled foods are generally advised to prevent overgrowth. Pectoral, dorsal, and anal fins are used for locomotion as the species lacks pelvic fins.

Like all members of Tetraodontidae, Arothron meleagris is capable of inflating its body by ingesting water when threatened. It should not be allowed to inhale air, as this can be fatal; a container rather than a net is generally recommended for transfers.

This species contains tetrodotoxin and is highly poisonous to eat; the toxin is also present in the skin and organs. Heavy filtration is generally advised given the species' messy eating habits. It has been reported to be aggressive toward conspecifics and toward similar-shaped species; most sources advise keeping it singly. Breeding has not yet been accomplished in captivity.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: The previous minVolumeLiters of 1300 L was an arithmetic average across incompatible data sets — hobby-retail minimums (~473–570 L) and specialist long-term-keep recommendations for fully grown adults (2000–3000 L). Averaging these produced a figure defensible by neither standard. Re-review of sources confirms: LiveAquaria (re-checked 2026-06-03) recommends 125 US gallons (~473 L); TFH Magazine recommends 150 US gallons (~568 L); ReefApp recommends 2000 L explicitly noting this is the volume needed for "a medium-size animal kept for several years" (i.e., a mature, fully grown specimen); Reeflex recommends 3000 L. The 2000–3000 L figures represent responsible long-term husbandry ideals for a 50 cm adult, not the practical minimum for acquiring a juvenile. The hobby consensus of 473–568 L represents the actual entry-level minimum seen in established trade sources. minVolumeLiters has been set to 500 L (nearest nice-values list entry to the hobby consensus range of 473–568 L). Aquarists are strongly advised to upgrade to the largest system practical as the fish grows toward its 50 cm adult size.
breedingNotes: Breeding has not been achieved in captivity. The species is oviparous in the wild. No captive breeding protocols are documented in the available literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific classification, adult size (50 cm), native range, temperature range (24.3–29°C), and diet. Temperature range differs from hobby sources (22.2–25.6°C from LiveAquaria/FreshMarine); averaged across all sources to produce final values of 23–27°C.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm), minimum tank size (2000 L), temperament, diet, and native range. ReefApp explicitly states 2000 L is the volume needed for "a medium-size animal kept for several years" — i.e., a long-term adult specimen. This is an aspirational/ideal figure rather than an entry-level minimum. Discrepancy with hobby-source minimums of 473–570 L is resolved in careNotes (2026-06-03).

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm), temperature range (24.3–29°C), minimum tank size (3000 L), care level (advanced), and toxicity notes. Tank size of 3000 L is the highest figure gathered; averaged across all 5 sources to produce 1300 L for minVolumeLiters.

additional.name: TFH Magazine
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm), minimum tank size (150 gal / 568 L), temperament (may harass non-robust tankmates), reef safety (not reef safe), and confirmed breeding not accomplished in captivity.

fish.breedingNotes: Not yet achieved in captivity. Oviparous in the wild.
